coredata.dc:title: Structuring biogenic carbon quantum dots integrated on biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 heterostructure for efficient electrocatalytic determination of endocrine disruptor Bisphenol-A
coredata.dc:creator: Srivastava, Ankur
Singh, Kshitij RB
Kumar, Praveen
Garg, Neha
Pandey, Mrituanjay D.
Singh, Jay
coredata.dc:description: In this manuscript, we report the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from the Aloe barbadensis miller (Aloe Vera) gel via the biogenic route. These CQDs were further used to stabilize biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs using the biogenic route to enhance the conductivity, similarly as can be achieved through the chemical doping of graphene oxide, which provides internal pi-pi electron conjugation. The objective of utilizing biogenic CQDs is to replace the toxic chemicals and provide the carbon source for obtaining the carbide, which can then associate with lanthanum oxide by the replacement of oxide ion from the C2 2− in acetylenic form. The prepared biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 heterostructure has been subjected to electrochemical studies. The polymorph has been also applied for electrochemical sensing of Bisphenol-A, a plastic monomer that functions as an endocrine disruptor. The sensitivity of fabricated Tyr/La2O3/La2O2C2/ITO bioelectrode is found 0.04276 mA/μM.cm2. and the efficient LOD value of the material is 3.06 × 10−2 μM. Furthermore, the polymorph is applied in the cell viability and internalization study. Moreover, to assist in the formation of compounds and obtain reliable results, the biphasic polymorphs have been characterized by structural, optical, and morphological characterization techniques.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The FT-IR spectra revealed the functional group information for the prepared nanomaterials for the CQD and biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs which is shown in Fig. 2(B), to compare the considerable changes which were obtained by FT-IR spectrophotometer Model- FT/IR-4700 JASCO, over the range of 400–4000 cm−1. A broad and highly intense peak was obtained at the 3363 cm−1 for the CQD corresponding to the –OH or –NH peak which appears shifted for the biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs and obtained at the 3424 cm−1. Further, the peak obtained at 1490 cm−1 correspond to asymmetric stretching mode for the C–O bond in biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs [30] whereas the sharp peak obtained at the 646 cm−1 was confirmed to the La–O stretching vibration which is attributed to reported literature [31]. The strong peak obtained in the case of CQD at 1642 cm−1 seems to merge, may correspond to the C[dbnd]C either C[dbnd]O whereas in the case of biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs the C[dbnd]O peak was found to be shifted, and obtained at 1727 cm−1. The peak obtained at 2356 cm−1 in the case of biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs due to the presence of carbonized in the C22− form, during the hydrothermal process which is supported by Raman spectroscopy, resemblance to reported literature [32] whereas the signals at 1600 and 1490 cm−1 are typical of carbon triple bond carbon skeletal stretching modes, which was supported by the work S. corrades et al., 2019 on the titanium carbide/carbon composite [33]. The confirmation of the acetylenic carbon was done by Raman spectroscopy given in the supplementary file, Figure [S1]. The peak obtained at the 2230 cm−1 corresponds to FT-IR spectroscopy [34].The transmittance electron microscopy also reveals the morphology of the materials which is taken from the instruments FEI Company of USA (S.E.A.) PTE, LTD model name, Tecnai G2 20 TWIN. Where the tiny CQD particles appear onto the lanthanum oxide nanomaterials. The irregular arrangements of the overall morphology obtained in the TEM images obtained at 100 nm, support the presence of polymorphic phase on the prepared materials that is shown in Fig. 6 (image D) and the zoom image taken at 50 nm is shown in image 6(E). The tiny-sized CQDs can be seen clearly where the three different types of fringes are present with three different d-spacing values which is appeared in Fig. 6(G), with the d-spacing value 3.29 Å, corresponding to the hkl (222) plane for the lanthanum oxide nanoparticles and carbon contained lanthanum oxide have d-spacing value 3.19 Å with the hkl (111) plane whereas La2O2C2 corresponds to 3.37 Å associated with (002) plane which is corresponds exactly with the powder XRD data. Moreover, the SAED pattern of the compound observed at 10 nm (image 6F) shows the crystalline nature of the material, and the average particle size was obtained using the image j software is 26 nm, Fig. 6(H).The authors, A.S. and K.R.B.S. would like to express their gratitude to Banaras Hindu University (BHU), Varanasi, for providing the necessary facilities to conduct the research work. Additionally, the authors acknowledge the SATHI facility, located at Banaras Hindu University's CDC building, for providing the confocal microscopy images. Furthermore, author J.S. and M.D.P. would like to acknowledge BHU for providing a seed grant and incentive grant respectively under the MoE Govt. India, Institute of Eminence (IoE) scheme.

The authors, A.S. and K.R.B.S. , would like to express their gratitude to Banaras Hindu University (BHU), Varanasi, for providing the necessary facilities to conduct the research work. ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this manuscript, we report the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from the Aloe barbadensis miller (Aloe Vera) gel via the biogenic route. These CQDs were further used to stabilize biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs using the biogenic route to enhance the conductivity, similarly as can be achieved through the chemical doping of graphene oxide, which provides internal pi-pi electron conjugation. The objective of utilizing biogenic CQDs is to replace the toxic chemicals and provide the carbon source for obtaining the carbide, which can then associate with lanthanum oxide by the replacement of oxide ion from the C2 2− in acetylenic form. The prepared biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 heterostructure has been subjected to electrochemical studies. The polymorph has been also applied for electrochemical sensing of Bisphenol-A, a plastic monomer that functions as an endocrine disruptor. The sensitivity of fabricated Tyr/La2O3/La2O2C2/ITO bioelectrode is found 0.04276 mA/μM.cm2. and the efficient LOD value of the material is 3.06 × 10−2 μM. Furthermore, the polymorph is applied in the cell viability and internalization study. Moreover, to assist in the formation of compounds and obtain reliable results, the biphasic polymorphs have been characterized by structural, optical, and morphological characterization techniques.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal oxide nanomaterials are considered to play a significant role in various applications within the research domain of materials science, engineering, and technology [1–3]. Nanomaterials, whether in the form of oxides or metallic compounds, have been widely utilized to enhance their physical, chemical, and biological properties. The performance of nanomaterials can be successfully improved through the incorporation of hetero atoms, either as nanohybrid or nanocomposites [4]. Another approach to enhance the physical and biological activity of the nanomaterials is through the surface modification of the inorganic nanocomposites, which can be achieved using various methods such as chemical treatments, grafting of synthetic polymers, and ligand exchange techniques [5].

Alternatively, mixed phase or multiphase nanomaterials are particularly interesting for various electrochemical applications including energy storage [6], sensor [7], and biosensor [8,9], due to their high electron transfer ability and variable oxidation state with multi-redox center [10,11]. In the past decades, rare earth metal oxide and their nanocomposites have been widely used in biomedical applications such as cancerous cell diagnosis and medicines [12]. The mixed-phase rare earth nanocomposites have found extensive usage in photocatalysis [13], batteries, electronics, optics [14], and high-performance supercapacitor. Z. Mohamad Riyas et al. prepared La2O3–NiO nanocomposites and investigated their effective synergetic effect, demonstrating good capacitive retention and long-term stability [15]. Similarly, silver-doped cerium lanthanum titanium oxide-based nanocomposites were reported by Olena mykolaivna Lavrynenka et al. for biomedical applications [16].

However, the incorporation of the conducting carbon using graphene oxide plays a vital role in the functional and morphological growth of the nanocomposites, due to its appreciable chemical stability, and large surface area [17]. Neodymium, first developed by A. Duane Buthers in 1966 in the form of Nd2O2C2 by physical method and arc-melting technique for the physical studies, was later incorporated with lanthanum oxide nanoparticles in 1972 to confine La2O2C2 from the atmospheric carbon monoxide using a slow passage of arc. This helped in understanding the thermodynamic properties of the compounds. Since there is limited research available on mixed-phase lanthanum oxide and lanthanum carbide nanocomposites, there is scope and interest in exploring this direction. The driving force to achieve the La2O2C2 phase is C2 2−, analogous to its oxide, which can replace the oxide ion with an acetylenic ion. The focus of this study is on the impact of biogenic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in the preparation of mixed-phase lanthanum oxide (La2O3) and lanthanum oxide carbide (La2O2C2) nanocomposites. Biogenic CQDs are environment friendly, avoiding use of toxic chemicals, and they can be easily prepared [18]. As Bony K. John et al. reported the fluorescent carbon quantum dots as dual sensor for the selective detection of Cr(VI) ions [19]. and N,S- doped CQDs was utilized as a sensor for azo dye tartrazine and fluorescent ink applications [20]. Moreover, CQDs have been widely used in various research applications. The role of CQDs is to serve as a carbon source in the synthesis of La2O2C2, acting as an alternative to graphene oxide. CQDs are prepared from biogenic methods using aloe vera gel (Aloe barbadensis miller), which contains various biochemical components such as alkaloids, terpenoids, flavonoids, carbohydrates, and other organic and inorganic compounds that work as stabilizing agents, capping agents and surfactants providing extra stability to the molecule. The elemental composition in the form of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen significantly participates in and affects the reaction rate, concentration, and composition [21,22].

Furthermore, the prepared mixed-phase nanocomposite was analyzed using various structural characterization techniques such as powder X-ray diffraction (XRD), and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). Morphological analysis was carried out using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmittance electron microscopy (TEM), and energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX). Additionally, optical characterizations were performed using UV–Visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. The biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs were further utilized for the electrochemical detection of the endocrine disruptor Bisphenol-A (2,2-bis(4-hydroxyphenyl) propane) (BPA) which was first reported by Dianin in 1891 [23]. BPA is known to cause hormonal imbalance and is responsible for the growth of cancer cells in mammals [24]. The prepared biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs exhibited a significant electrochemical sensing response to BPA as compared to previously reported works and other electrochemical studies, such as scan rate, cyclic voltammetry (CV), and pH-based studies. The polymorphs also demonstrated long-term reusability and quick response time. The prepared compound could be observed very sensitive as every small change in the concertation, compound provided effective response towards analytes. An appreciable LOD value of 3.06 × 10−2 μM was observed for this compound. Moreover, the compound was very selective for the BPA, which shows negligible interference with other interferents analytes. The prepared polymorphs were further explored for cytotoxicity and life cell imaging using the RAW cell line. The experiments showed negligible toxic effects on the cell line and the material was found effectively permeated the cells. This study aims to lay the groundwork for future in vitro and in vivo studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of aloe vera gel (Aloe barbadensis miller) extract
ce:section.ce:para: Aloe barbadensis miller leaves were meticulously cleaned of any contaminants for this study using Milli-Q water. The semi-solid translucent gel was extracted from the leaves after washing. After that, the gel was carefully cut into smaller pieces. The gel's original weight before to extraction was determined to be 150 gm. The chopped gel was filtered to create the gel solution, and the filtrate was then collected using Whatman filter paper. In order to preserve its integrity for upcoming experiments and analysis, the filtrate solution, which contained the components recovered from the Aloe gel, was carefully refrigerated at 4 °C.

The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using a hydrothermal method from the natural extract of Aloe barbadensis miller gel. The synthesis process involved several steps. Firstly, the Aloe barbadensis miller gel extract was sonicated for 40 min to ensure proper dispersion. The sonicated extract was then transferred into a 100 ml Teflon autoclave. The autoclave containing the gel extract was placed in a muffle furnace and maintained at a temperature of 180 °C for 24 h. This process resulted in the formation of a blackish solution. After the hydrothermal process, the pellet formed in the solution was discarded and supernatant was subjected to a purification process via centrifugation at 8000 rpm with water and ethanol-water as solvents. The yield of the CQDs prepared in this work is 57 %.

The biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs was prepared using Hydrothermal methods. Firstly, 0.1 M aqueous solution of La(NO3)3.6H2O in 30 ml Milli-Q water was treated with the 5 M solution of NaOH to achieve the solution of 12 pH. Subsequently, 30 ml of previously prepared CQDs, was added to the above solution. The resulting solution was transferred to the Teflon-lined hydrothermal autoclave which was placed inside the muffle furnace at 180 °C temperature for 24 h. After the completion of the reaction the reaction was allowed to cool at room temperature. Further it was centrifuged at 10000 rpm and then washed several times with the Milli-Q water and mixture of ethanol-water. The compound was dried in the vacuum oven at the 60 °C for the further characterizations. The plausible reaction steps for the formation of lanthanum oxide & lanthanum carbide nanoparticles is presented in Eqs. (1)–(3 ), based on previously reported literature [25,26]. A schematic representation of overall procedure involved for the preparation of biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs is given in Fig. 1 for the preparation of biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs.

Raw 264.7 cells were acquired from the National Cell Line repository, National Center for Cell Science (NCCS) Pune, Maharashtra India. Cells were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM)-high glucose, supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum, 2 mM glutamine, 1 % antibiotics in a humidified 5 % CO2 environment at 37 °C. Cells were passed upon 80 % confluence.

Raw 264.7 cells were seeded at a density of 5000 cells per well in 100 μl of DMEM. Cells were allowed to grow overnight in the CO2 incubator. After 12h, cells were incubated with various concentration of the biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorph (0, 3.125, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, 100 μg/ml) for 48h. Cell viability in various La2O3/La2O2C2 concentrations was determined using 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) method [27]. Briefly, cells were incubated with 10 μL of the MTT (5 mg/ml stock) for 2h at in a CO2 incubator at 37 °C. Media was discarded and formazon crystals formed after conversion from MTT inside the cells were dissolved in 100 μL of DMSO. Absorbance of was measured at 570 nm on microplate reader from biotech.

1X10 [5] cells per well were seeded in a 6-well plate. Cells were allowed to grow overnight. Cells were treated with either biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorph (20 μg/ml) for 2 h’ minutes in the CO2 chamber of a live cell imaging confocal microscope from Leica. Cells were washed thrice with warm 1X PBS followed by imaging Leica microscope for live imaging at excitation. 300 nm and emission 415 nm wavelength.
The detailed explanation of the material used and characterization techniques is given in the supplementary file.

The powder XRD has been done for the prepared CQD and biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs separately to reveal out crystalline nature and size of the materials which was recorded by X-ray diffractometer, the model's name of which is Bruker, D8 Advance of Cu–K@ radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å), 2θ angles ranging from 10o to 80o. The XRD of CQD having hkl value 002 corresponds to JCPDS card number 41–1487 which has primitive type lattice with the cell parameter a = 2.47 and c = 6.72. Some spicks are also appearing in the spectra of CQDs, which may be some heteroatoms from the biogenic synthesis. in the whereas the nanocomposite La2O3 & La2O2C2 corresponds to the JCPDS card no 70–0231 and 65–3185, having highest intensity 2θ value and hkl plane at 28 (111) and 27 (222) respectively Fig. 2 (A) The crystalline nature of the La2O2C2 was obtained monoclinic having end centered lattice with the lattice parameter a = 7.06, b = 3.98 and c = 7.31 where β = 95.70. Moreover, the crystalline nature of the La2O3 was found cubic having body centered arrangement with the cell parameter a = 11.42. The related d-spacing value at the highest intensity 2θ value for the La2O2C2 and La2O3 was found 3.19 Å and 3.29 Å respectively. The average crystalline size was calculated by applying the Scherrer equation [28] ( Eq. (4) )
4

Where D is the average crystalline size, K is the Scherrer constant (0.9), λ (1.54) is the wavelength applied for the diffraction, and full width at half maxima β in radians. The value of average crystalline size was found to be 0.16 nm for CQDs and 10.15 nm for La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs. There was some extra peak was also found in the XRD graph of CQD which attributed due to the cause of biogenic synthetic procedure of CQD where various functional group were present in the form of alkaloids, terpenoids and amino acids, and carbohydrates [29]. The powder XRD results reveal that the prepared nanocomposite consisting the both La2O3 and La2O2C2 properties and indicated the successful incorporation of C2 2− provided by CQD as a carbon source to the lanthanum oxide nanoparticles during the course of the reaction. The carbon elements in the La2O2C2 exist in acetylenic from which provided better functionality and stability to the material. and two π bonds present in C2 2− carbide ion in generated more conducting ability in the materials.

The FT-IR spectra revealed the functional group information for the prepared nanomaterials for the CQD and biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs which is shown in Fig. 2(B), to compare the considerable changes which were obtained by FT-IR spectrophotometer Model- FT/IR-4700 JASCO, over the range of 400–4000 cm−1. A broad and highly intense peak was obtained at the 3363 cm−1 for the CQD corresponding to the –OH or –NH peak which appears shifted for the biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs and obtained at the 3424 cm−1. Further, the peak obtained at 1490 cm−1 correspond to asymmetric stretching mode for the C–O bond in biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs [30] whereas the sharp peak obtained at the 646 cm−1 was confirmed to the La–O stretching vibration which is attributed to reported literature [31]. The strong peak obtained in the case of CQD at 1642 cm−1 seems to merge, may correspond to the CC either CO whereas in the case of biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs the CO peak was found to be shifted, and obtained at 1727 cm−1. The peak obtained at 2356 cm−1 in the case of biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs due to the presence of carbonized in the C2 2− form, during the hydrothermal process which is supported by Raman spectroscopy, resemblance to reported literature [32] whereas the signals at 1600 and 1490 cm−1 are typical of carbon triple bond carbon skeletal stretching modes, which was supported by the work S. corrades et al., 2019 on the titanium carbide/carbon composite [33]. The confirmation of the acetylenic carbon was done by Raman spectroscopy given in the supplementary file, Figure [S1]. The peak obtained at the 2230 cm−1 corresponds to FT-IR spectroscopy [34].

The optical properties were explored by UV–Visible and Fluorescence spectroscopy for the CQDs and biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs. The UV–Visible spectroscopy was obtained by UV-1800 instrument (Shimadzu, Japan), from 200 to 800 nm wavelength range shown in Fig. 3 (A), and photoluminescence spectra was taken from the instrument. Horiba Fluoromax 4CP plus spectrophotometer with a 10 quartz cells at 25 °C. The absorption spectra were obtained at 280 nm and a slight hump around 362 nm due to π-π* and n-π* transition respectively was found nearly similar results corresponding to the reported literature by Raj Kumar Bandi et al., 2013, where they reported 280 nm and 260 nm for the UV absorption spectra for the CQDs [35]. Whereas the UV- absorption spectra for La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs show blue shift due to the quantum confinement which was found at 300 nm and a very slight hump at around 450 nm. Guguloth Ravi et al. reported the doped La2O3 nanoparticles show the absorbance at the 250–310 nm [36] and resemblance with the La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorph. The bend gap was also calculated from the obtained UV graph, found 3.6 and 3.9 eV shown in Fig. 3(C and D).

The emission spectra were recorded for the CQDs at the excitation wavelength 280 nm and 300 nm for La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs which is shown in Fig. 3(B). The emission spectra were found sharp peak at 415 nm due to be an electronic structure having an unfilled 4f subshell and the energy transfer by the ground state level of doubly ionized lanthanum is 5 d2D3/2 which is excited to higher energy levels [37] whereas, the CQDs was found a slightly broader peak at 384, 430, and 470 nm which was found a similar result with the reported literature [38].

The TEM images of CQDs shows the flower bunches like morphology given in Fig. 4 (A) and B, the 2D and 3D images of the AFM also consisted the small bunch like arrangements. Fig. 4(C) and D. In the height parameters the Root mean square roughness has been found 2.62 nm, average roughness was found 2.0 nm and maximum area peak height was found 17.88. The average particle size has been calculated from the TEM image, using image j software which was found 6.35 nm (Fig. 4(E)). confirm the quantum sized particles of CQDs. The hydrodynamic radii were obtained from the DLS analysis which was found 266.41 nm and the solvation sphere size was obtained 260.06 nm. The Zeta potential was obtained with negative potential, −15.15 nm, indicate the CQDs are negatively charged (Fig. 4(F) and G).

Elemental analysis was performed to examine the chemical state of biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs by irradiation of X-rays, resulting emission of photoelectron energies were analyzed for particular elements within the sampling volume which can be seen in the survey image Fig. 5 (A). The binding energies of C1s fitted positions, Fig. 5(C), are obtained at the 283.1 eV, 284.6 eV, and 287.5 eV which represents the C–C, C–O/-OH and –COO– respectively where binding energy 283.1, 284.6 eV and 287.5 eV exactly corresponds to previously reported literature [39].

The O 1s core level spectra are given in Fig. 5(D), obtained the binding energy (BE) for various fitted peaks where, Anuja A. Yadav et al. reported the lanthanum oxide nanoparticles observed the binding energy obtained at the 528.1 and 530.6 eV attributed to the La–O bonds and hydroxyl group contained species respectively [40] and Travis E Jones el al. 2015 shows agreement with obtained results [41]. The elemental analysis after the peak fitting for oxygen corresponds to the previously reported work, where the La–O exist at the lower binding energy component and the band obtained at the 530.6 eV hydroxyl-carbonated or carbonated species. The lanthanum-based compound having (CO3)−2 and OH− group is allowed to be the intermediate form of hydroxyl carbonate. The formation of this type of intermediate can be formed from the atmospheric agent with the lanthanum oxide surface which is reported in the previous literature [42,43].

The core La3d shows two doublet peaks for 3d3/2 and 3d5/2 obtained at 850.3–853.3 and 833–836.4 eV respectively showing the La3+ oxidation state having the energy gap 20.3eV. These core level 3d5/2 binding energy values responsible to the identification of the chemical state lanthanum in oxide form. From the reported literature the La 3d5/2 corresponds to the 836.4 eV [32], and 832.9 eV [44] was found similar corresponding peak for the 3d5/2 for the lanthanum in oxide form shown in Fig. 5(B), these peaks represent as characteristics peak for the oxide of lanthanum whereas the binding energy peak at 850.3 and 853.3 eV corresponds to the La 3d3/2. The Thota Trinadh also reported the binding energy for La, 3d3/2 at the range 851.8–853.8 eV [45] and it was found to be a slight deviation from the reported literature may be due to the different chemical environment in both cases.

The surface morphology was analyzed from the SEM instrument, Carl Zeiss, EVO-18, Research Model, Germany, and appears cloudy and fibrous containing lanthanum oxide and carbon quantum dots. The reported literature the Jiaoxia Zhang et al. also reported the graphene/nanocomposite and obtained likewise fibrous and layered morphology [39]. SEM images having some spherical ball-like arrangements are presented which is shown in Fig. 6 (A). The La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorph has surface folds and layered structure where the lanthanum and carbon nanoparticles are uniformly intermixed with each other, which supports the formation of La2O2C2 nanocomposite's structure. From Fig. 6(B) obtained at 200 nm, different shapes of particles appear which suggest the mixed morphological arrangements of particles. It is obvious from Fig. 6(C), only lanthanum, carbon, and oxygen are presents in bulk, and remaining some small peaks are observed due to the presence of some bio-compound from the biogenetically synthesized CQDs.

The transmittance electron microscopy also reveals the morphology of the materials which is taken from the instruments FEI Company of USA (S.E.A.) PTE, LTD model name, Tecnai G2 20 TWIN. Where the tiny CQD particles appear onto the lanthanum oxide nanomaterials. The irregular arrangements of the overall morphology obtained in the TEM images obtained at 100 nm, support the presence of polymorphic phase on the prepared materials that is shown in Fig. 6 (image D) and the zoom image taken at 50 nm is shown in image 6(E). The tiny-sized CQDs can be seen clearly where the three different types of fringes are present with three different d-spacing values which is appeared in Fig. 6(G), with the d-spacing value 3.29 Å, corresponding to the hkl (222) plane for the lanthanum oxide nanoparticles and carbon contained lanthanum oxide have d-spacing value 3.19 Å with the hkl (111) plane whereas La2O2C2 corresponds to 3.37 Å associated with (002) plane which is corresponds exactly with the powder XRD data. Moreover, the SAED pattern of the compound observed at 10 nm (image 6F) shows the crystalline nature of the material, and the average particle size was obtained using the image j software is 26 nm, Fig. 6(H).

The three-dimensional analysis of surface morphology was revealed through atomic force microscopy (AFM), which is unique to give the z-axis analysis along with two (x, & y) dimensions, recorded by the instruments namely NEXT, NT-MDT. To perform the AFM, the sample was prepared on an ITO glass surface through an electrophoretic deposition technique which was performed with and without the enzymatic deposition to analyze the morphological change after the enzyme deposition. The root mean square roughness (Rms-Rq), average roughness (Ra), and maximum area peak height were carried out for the La2O3/La2O2C2/ITO electrode and Tyr/La2O3/La2O2C2/ITO electrode, which is given in Table 1 .

It was observed that after the enzyme deposition, Rms-Rq, Ra as well as maximum area peak height value was found much high for the sample as compared to those without enzyme deposition which is due to the enzymatic functional group may provide the higher roughness and height, is the sign of appreciable interaction between the materials and enzymes which is responsible for the better catalytic activity. Moreover, average roughness (Ra) was also found about 12 times higher value with the enzymatic sample, which inform to be increased surface area of the electrode, is essential for the surface activity in the electrocatalysis reactions. However, the morphological arrangement for La2O3/La2O2C2/ITO electrode, Fig. 7 (A) and B, was found somewhat granular shaped some of them are large but some of them are very tiny particles. The large granules appear mostly on one of the axes whereas small granules are adsorbed on that surface may be La2O2C2 particles associated with lanthanum oxide nanoparticles. The morphological arrangements for the Tyr/La2O3/La2O2C2/ITO electrode are shown in Fig. 7(C) and D, where some zig-zag arrangements were formed with the materials and enzyme.

The appropriate current response of the cyclic voltammetry for the prepared compound can be obtained at a specific pH for a particular compound. To get analyze the optimum pH the CV response was optimized for the Tyr/La2O3/La2O2C2/ITO electrode at various pH ranges from 5.7 to 7.5 and it was observed that at 6.5 pH the optimum anodic peak (I
pa ) current was found that is 0.7134, for which the 50 mM phosphate buffer solution having 5 mM [Fe (CN)6]3-/4- including 0.9 % saline solution and the scan rate was taken 50 mV s−1. Fig. 8 (A), It is obvious from the pH graph the oxidative peak current response was observed in a zig-jag manner concerning the pH 5.7, 6.0, 6.5, 7.0, and 7.5. The optimum current was found at 6.5 pH and the minimum current was found at the 7.5. that's why we choose pH 6.5 for further electrochemical experiments.

The scan rate study was performed for the Tyr/La2O3/La2O2C2/ITO bioelectrode to analyze the interfacial kinetics at the different scan rate varying from 10 to 100 mV s−1 Fig. 8 (C &D) which reveals the information for the magnitude of oxidative/anodic (I
pa ) and reductive/cathodic (I
pc ) peak current respectively. The result shows that I
pa and I
pc values followed linear pattern by increasing the scan rate Fig. 8(C). Further, the study also gives information for the shifting in the I
pa current towards a more positive value and shifting in the I
pc current towards a more negative value, suggesting the quasi-reversible process for the oxidation-reduction behavior Fig. 8(D). An apparent linear enhancement has been found in I
pa and I
pc values in the obtained plot of, the current vs. square root of the scan rate, having the R2 value 0.99 for both anodic and cathodic peaks respectively.

The value of charge transfer rate constant (Ks) was determined from the surface modification of the Tyr/La2O3/La2O2C2/ITO bioelectrode has been calculated by applying Eq. (5) [47], where gas constant R is 8.314 J mol−1 K−1, Faraday constant F is 96485 C mol−1, the separation of the peak-to-peak (V) is m and n is the number of electrons transferred during the reaction, is 1; T is room temperature (300 K) and the scan rate ν is 50 mV. The K
s was calculated by using the above values for the Tyr/La2O3/La2O2C2/ITO bioelectrode is 1.06 s−1, allocate to the more catalytic activity of the material. It has been observed from the potentials shift [(ΔE
p
= E
pa (anodic peak) – E
pc (cathodic peak)], a linear relationship has been obtained concerning scan rate which shows the electrochemical reaction of tyrosinase with La2O3/La2O2C2 nanoparticles, was diffusion-controlled phenomena. Further, the Randles-Sevcik equation Eq. (6) [48], calculating the diffusion coefficient applied for the Tyr/La2O3/La2O2C2/ITO bioelectrode surface, from the electrolyte solution of [Fe(CN)6)]3-/4- where, I
p denote electrode peak current, the surface area (A) of the electrode is 0.25 cm2, the number of the electron (n) is 1 and the concentration (C) is 5 mM at the surface, applying these value in Eq. (3) [9], the D value has been obtained 0.299 cm2 s−1. The Brown-Anson model [7] ( Eq. (6) ) [49], has been applied for the surface concentration of the electrode where γ stands for the surface concentration of the electrode (mol cm−2), the number of the electron (n) is 1, and the room temperature (T). Thus for the Tyr/La2O3/La2O2C2/ITO bioelectrode the surface concentration has been obtained by putting this value in Eq. (7) to be 4.31 × 10−7 mol cm−2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical response study of Bisphenol- A
ce:section.ce:para:
Bisphenol-A sensing study was carried out in the form of electrochemical CV analysis in the 50 mM PBS adding the 5 mM [Fe (CN)6]3-/4- with the 0.9 % saline solution. The study reveals that on increasing the analyte concentration range from 0 μM to 32 μM shown in Fig. 9 (A), the oxidative peak current (I
pa ) and reductive peak current (I
pc ) increases and decreases respectively in a linear manner having the linearity of 0–32 μM, where the linear regression coefficient for oxidative and reductive peak current was found 0.991 and 0.990 respectively ( Eqs. 8 & 9 ; Fig. 9(B)). Moreover, the limit of detection was calculated from the slope obtained from the curve between the current in mA and concentration vs. potential by using the formula LOD = 3 × SD/Sensitivity, where the SD stands for standard deviation. However, the sensitivity was found from the ratio of slope and active surface area (0.25 cm2), which was calculated as 0.04276 mA/μM.cm2 and the LOD was found to be 3.06 × 10−2 μM. The enzyme activity was found in the form of Michalis Menten constant (Km) value obtained, −4.34 μM, Fig. 9(C), with the linear regression coefficient value 0.99 ( Eq. (10)
), which shows the appreciable enzymatic activity of the prepared electrode with analyte Bisphenol-A, fabricated with tyrosinase enzyme. The simultaneous activity of prepared biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs provided the extra conductivity due to the presence of a bimetallic system of lanthanum and copper metal of enzyme with the carbon in the quantum dot reason, responsible for appreciable result in the electrochemical oxido-reductive process. Moreover, the biological route also provided better functionality with the material as well as an enzyme.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response time, reusability and selectivity analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Response time study provides suitable periods at which the activity of the prepared electrode is maximum. To obtain appropriate current that time must be required for a particular prepared material for which the CV was performed in the 50 mM phosphate buffer solution having 5 mM [Fe (CN)6]3-/4- with the 0.9 % saline solution Fig. 10 (A). The CV was taken from 0 to 65 s time interval and it was observed that the oxidative current value was found to be increased up to 35 s and remain constant up to 45 s but on further increasing the time interval the current was again increased which was found to be optimum at 50 s. Thus, 50 s was considered as the appropriate response time for the prepared electrode.

The human blood sample contains several bio-components which can affect simultaneously the standard observing analyte Fig. 11 . To observe the interferents electrochemical response with the biological components the CV was carried out in the same buffer solution where ascorbic acid (AA), glucose (Glu), lactic acid (LA), uric acid (UA), glutamic acid (GA) and mixture was taken as interferents analyte. The percent RSD value was calculated for the given in Table 2
, where the other interferents were found not interrupting with the standard BPA analyte. In addition, to provide a comparative overview of this work with the other pre-existing studies see Table 3.

Raw 264.7 cells were seeded at a density of 5000 cells per well in 100 μl of DMEM. Cells were allowed to grow overnight in the CO2 incubator. After 12h, cells were incubated with various concentration of the biphasic polymorph La2O3/La2O2C2 (0, 3.125, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, 100 μg/ml) for 48h (Fig. 12 ). Cell viability in various concentrations was determined using 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) method. Briefly, cells were incubated with 10 μL of the MTT (5 mg/ml stock) for 2h at in a CO2 incubator at 37 °C. Media was discarded and formazon crystals formed after conversion from MTT inside the cells were dissolved in 100 μL of DMSO. Absorbance of was measured at 570 nm on microplate reader from Biotech.

Cell viability of the biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorph was determined using MTT assay method. We found that La2O3/La2O2C2 were non-toxic in the various concentration ranges tested Fig. 12. We treated Raw cells with 20 μg/ml for our live cell imaging experiments, which was nontoxic. After 2 h of incubation the cells showed internalization of La2O3/La2O2C2 inside the cells Fig. 13 . Internalization study demonstrates that the La
2
O
3
/La
2
O
2
C
2 can be internalized by the living cells and can be detected. This observation can be suitable exploited in a number of biological applications.

The biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs were successfully synthesized using CQDs as carbon source reagents, prepared from the biogenic route with the aloe vera via hydrothermal method. The biological route avoids the use of hazardous chemicals and their byproducts reducing environmental waste. The prepared compound was examined using powder XRD, FT-IR, XPS, UV–Visible, and fluorescence spectroscopy. Characteristic peaks and changes in spectra were observed. The powder XRD provided the corresponding hkl values, and the average crystalline size was approximately 10 nm. In FT-IR spectroscopy, characteristic peaks for the La–O bond and carbonic bond were observed, and XPS analysis supported the elemental oxidation state and electronic environment of the material. The optical spectra were consistent with previously reported absorbance and emission spectra. The morphology of the compounds was analyzed using SEM, TEM, AFM, and EDAX. The 3D morphological arrangements were observed in the form of Rms-rq, Ra, and maximum area peak height. The roughness parameters values are 10.43, 8.26, and 50.37 without using the enzyme tyrosinase and 125.49, 101.74, and 450.17 with the enzyme. Moreover, the 2D morphological arrangements obtained from the SEM, and TEM micrographs, showed cloudy and tiny-sized morphology closely related to each other. The HR-TEM images revealed three different arrangements representing different phases of the prepared compounds, with distinct fringes. SEAD images confirmed the crystalline nature of the prepared biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs. The d-spacing values such as 3.29 (222) and 3.19 (111) hkl, matched exactly with the SEAD image and powder XRD data. The average particle size, calculated using image-j software was found 26 nm.

Furthermore, the prepared compound has been used for electrochemical detection in phosphate buffer solution. pH, CV, and scan rate studies were carried out. The kinetics study has been carried out from the scan rate analysis. The surface concentration value for this compound was determined to be 4.31 × 10−7 mol cm−2. The diffusion coefficient value was measured at 0.299 cm2 s−1. The charge transfer rate constant value is 1.06 s−1 indicating high catalytic activity. The electrochemical sensing of Bisphenol-A has been performed using cyclic voltammetry within a concentration range of 0–32 μM in the PBS at pH 6.5. The sensitivity of the compound was determined to be 0.04276 mA/μM.cm2, and the LOD was found to be 3.06 × 10−2 μM which is more efficient compared to other reported works. The compound demonstrated an effective response time of 50 s and good reusability over a period of 20 days. The obtained Km value for enzyme kinetics was −4.34 μM. The selectivity analysis was conducted using different analytes in the same phosphate buffer solution resulting negligible % RSD value. Moreover, the cell viability of the compound was assessed using the MTT assay method, with RAW cells, and the compound showed non-toxic nature within the various concentration ranges. The compound was found to be internalized by living cells based on internalization studies. The prepared biphasic La2O3/La2O2C2 polymorphs were found to be highly suitable for establishing biphasic nanomaterials and for the selective detection of Bisphenol-A. The compound also holds promise for future in vitro and in vivo studies.
